1959 film by Alfred Hitchcock

For other uses, see [North by Northwest (disambiguation)](/wiki/North_by_Northwest_(disambiguation) "North by Northwest (disambiguation)").

***North by Northwest*** is a 1959 American [spy](/wiki/Spy_film "Spy film") [thriller film](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") produced and directed by [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), starring [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant"), [Eva Marie Saint](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint"), and [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason"). The original screenplay written by [Ernest Lehman](/wiki/Ernest_Lehman "Ernest Lehman") was intended to be the basis for "the Hitchcock picture to end all Hitchcock pictures".[[3]](#cite_note-Jaynes-Trach-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

*North by Northwest* is a tale of mistaken identity: an innocent man (Grant) is pursued across the United States by agents of a mysterious organization that aims to prevent him from blocking their plan to smuggle microfilm containing government secrets out of the country. It is one of several Hitchcock films featuring a musical score by [Bernard Herrmann](/wiki/Bernard_Herrmann "Bernard Herrmann") and an opening title sequence by graphic designer [Saul Bass](/wiki/Saul_Bass "Saul Bass"). The film was the first to feature extended use of [kinetic typography](/wiki/Kinetic_typography "Kinetic typography") in its opening credits.

The film was released on July 1, 1959, to critical and commercial success. It topped the box office in the United States for [seven consecutive weeks](/wiki/List_of_1959_box_office_number-one_films_in_the_United_States "List of 1959 box office number-one films in the United States"). The film was nominated for three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") and garnered Hitchcock the [Silver Shell for Best Director](/wiki/Silver_Shell_for_Best_Director "Silver Shell for Best Director") at the [San Sebastián Film Festival](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_International_Film_Festival "San Sebastián International Film Festival").

It is listed among the canonical Hitchcock films of the 1950s and is widely regarded among the  [greatest films ever made](/wiki/List_of_films_voted_the_best "List of films voted the best").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1995, the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") selected *North by Northwest* for preservation in the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") for being "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

A waiter pages George Kaplan at the [Oak Room restaurant](/wiki/Oak_Room_(Plaza_Hotel) "Oak Room (Plaza Hotel)") at the [Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Plaza_Hotel "Plaza Hotel") in New York City at the request of two well-dressed men. As advertising executive Roger Thornhill summons the same waiter, he is mistaken for Kaplan, kidnapped by the pair, and brought to the estate of Lester Townsend in [Glen Cove](/wiki/Glen_Cove,_New_York "Glen Cove, New York"). Townsend interrogates Thornhill and subsequently arranges to kill him in a staged [drunk-driving](/wiki/Drunk_driving "Drunk driving") crash; Thornhill survives, but fails to convince the police or his mother of what happened. Returning to the estate, Thornhill learns that Townsend is a [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") diplomat.

Thornhill and his mother sneak into Kaplan's empty room at the Plaza, where the thugs have followed him. Thornhill heads to the U.N. [General Assembly Building](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Building "United Nations General Assembly Building") to meet Townsend, who turns out to be a completely different man. As he asks him about the man impersonating him, one of the gang's thugs throws a knife into Townsend's back. Townsend collapses in Thornhill's arms, and Thornhill is photographed grabbing at the knife, giving the appearance that he is the murderer. Thornhill flees, attempting to find the real Kaplan.

The "United States Intelligence Agency" (USIA) realizes that Thornhill has been mistaken for Kaplan, a non-existent agent they created as a decoy to distract their quarries, but its chief, "the Professor", decides against rescuing him for fear of compromising their operation.

Thornhill sneaks aboard the luxurious *[20th Century Limited](/wiki/20th_Century_Limited "20th Century Limited")* train to Chicago without a ticket, where he meets Eve Kendall, who hides him from the police in her stateroom's upper berth. The two establish a relationship—on Kendall's part because she is secretly working with the federal agents—and she helps him elude a police dragnet. She then tells Thornhill that she has arranged a meeting with Kaplan at a rural bus stop in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"). When Thornhill arrives there, he is attacked by a [crop duster](/wiki/Aerial_application "Aerial application") armed with a machine gun. After being flushed from cover in a cornfield, he attempts to halt a passing [tanker truck](/wiki/Tanker_truck "Tanker truck"); the airplane crashes into it and both explode. In the ensuing confusion he steals a bystander's pickup truck. At Kaplan's hotel in Chicago, Thornhill learns that Kaplan had checked out before the time when Kendall claimed to have spoken with him. He goes to her room and confronts her, but she eludes him.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_%283%29.jpg/250px-North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_(3).jpg)

Roger Thornhill, Eve Kendall, and Phillip Vandamm at the Mount Rushmore visitor center

Thornhill tracks Kendall to an art auction, where the Townsend-impersonator—Phillip Vandamm, the ringleader of the group—is purchasing a small primitive statue. Vandamm instructs his henchmen to deal with Thornhill. Thornhill disrupts the auction until police are called to remove him. He confesses to them that he is the fugitive murderer, but the Professor intervenes. At the airport, he tells Thornhill that Kaplan was fictitious and that Eve Kendall is their real agent. Vandamm lives above [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore"), and the agency thinks he will be leaving the country by plane from there. Thornhill agrees to help maintain Kendall's cover.

At the Mount Rushmore visitor center, now playing the role of Kaplan at the USIA's request, Thornhill negotiates for Vandamm's turnover of Kendall to be arrested. Kendall shoots Thornhill with blanks and flees. Afterward, the Professor arranges for Thornhill and Kendall to meet; Thornhill learns that Kendall is to depart on the plane with Vandamm and his right-hand man Leonard. He tries to dissuade her from going, but is knocked unconscious by the Professor's driver and locked in a hospital room.

Thornhill escapes and goes to [Vandamm's home](/wiki/Vandamm_House "Vandamm House") to rescue Kendall, where he overhears that the sculpture holds [microfilm](/wiki/Microform#Microfilm "Microform") and that Leonard has discovered the blanks in Kendall's gun. Vandamm indicates that he will dispose of Kendall by throwing her from the plane; Thornhill manages to warn her with a surreptitious note. Vandamm, Leonard, and Kendall head for the plane. Thornhill is momentarily held at gunpoint by the housekeeper until he realizes she is holding Kendall's gun loaded with blanks. As Vandamm boards, Kendall takes the sculpture and runs to the pursuing Thornhill, and they flee to the top of Mount Rushmore. As they climb down, they are pursued by Vandamm's men. A park ranger fatally shoots Leonard, and Vandamm is taken into custody by the Professor.

As Kendall hangs on by her fingertips, Thornhill reaches down to pull her up. She is next seen—as Mrs. Thornhill—being pulled into an upper berth on a train, which suggestively enters a tunnel.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Writing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_North_by_Northwest_Original_Still.jpg)

Cary Grant in an original still for the film

Hitchcock often told journalists of an idea that he had about [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant") hiding from the villains inside [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s nose and being given away when he sneezes. He speculated that the film could be called *The Man on Lincoln's Nose*.[[14]](#cite_note-brady201-14) Hitchcock sat on the idea, waiting for the right screenwriter to develop it. The original traveling salesman character had been suited to [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart"), but Lehman changed it to a Madison Avenue advertising executive, a position that he had formerly held.[[15]](#cite_note-engel-15)

[John Russell Taylor](/wiki/John_Russell_Taylor "John Russell Taylor")'s 1978 biography *Hitch: The Life and Times of Alfred Hitchcock* suggests that the story originated after a spell of writer's block during the scripting of another film project:

> Alfred Hitchcock had agreed to do a film for MGM and they had chosen an adaptation of the novel *[The Wreck of the Mary Deare](/wiki/The_Wreck_of_the_Mary_Deare "The Wreck of the Mary Deare")* by [Hammond Innes](/wiki/Hammond_Innes "Hammond Innes"). Composer [Bernard Herrmann](/wiki/Bernard_Herrmann "Bernard Herrmann") had recommended that Hitchcock work with his friend [Ernest Lehman](/wiki/Ernest_Lehman "Ernest Lehman"). After a couple of weeks, Lehman offered to quit saying he didn't know what to do with the story. Hitchcock told him they got along great together and they would just write something else. Lehman said that he wanted to make the ultimate Hitchcock film. Hitchcock thought for a moment then said he had always wanted to do a chase across [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore"). Lehman and Hitchcock brainstormed more ideas: a murder at the United Nations headquarters; a murder at a car plant in Detroit; a final showdown in Alaska. Eventually they settled on the U.N. murder for the opening and the chase across Mount Rushmore for the climax. For the central idea, Hitchcock remembered something an American journalist had told him about spies creating a fake agent as a decoy. Perhaps their hero could be mistaken for this fictitious agent and end up on the run. They bought the idea from the journalist for $10,000.

Lehman repeated this story in the documentary *[Destination Hitchcock: The Making of North by Northwest](/wiki/Destination_Hitchcock:_The_Making_of_North_by_Northwest "Destination Hitchcock: The Making of North by Northwest")* that accompanied the 2001 DVD release of the film. Screenwriter [William Goldman](/wiki/William_Goldman "William Goldman") insisted in *[Which Lie Did I Tell?](/wiki/Which_Lie_Did_I_Tell%3F "Which Lie Did I Tell?")* that it was Lehman who created *North by Northwest* and that many of Hitchcock's ideas were not used. Hitchcock had the idea of the hero being stranded in the middle of nowhere but suggested that the villains try to kill him with a [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"). "But *they're* trying to kill him. How are *they* going to work up a cyclone?" Lehman responded. "I just can't tell you who said what to whom, but somewhere during that afternoon, the cyclone in the sky became the crop-duster plane."[[16]](#cite_note-brady202-16)

Hitchcock had been working on the story for nearly nine years prior to meeting Lehman.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Otis Guernsey](/wiki/Otis_Guernsey_Jr. "Otis Guernsey Jr.") was the American journalist who had the idea which influenced Hitchcock, inspired by a true story during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when [British Intelligence](/wiki/British_Intelligence "British Intelligence") obtained a dead body, invented a [fictitious officer](/wiki/Operation_Mincemeat#Major_William_Martin,_Royal_Marines "Operation Mincemeat") who was carrying secret papers, and arranged for the body and misleading papers to be discovered by the Germans as a disinformation scheme called [Operation Mincemeat](/wiki/Operation_Mincemeat "Operation Mincemeat"). Guernsey turned his idea into a story about an American salesman who travels to the Middle East and is mistaken for a fictitious agent, becoming "saddled with a romantic and dangerous identity".

Guernsey admitted that his treatment was full of "corn" and "lacking logic", and he urged Hitchcock to do what he liked with the story. Hitchcock bought the 60 pages for $10,000.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In an interview in the book *Screenwriters on Screenwriting* (1995), Lehman stated that he had already written much of the screenplay before coming up with critical elements of the climax. An example of the "corn" in the finished screenplay was the scene where Roger Thornhill returns to the Townsend estate with the detectives to find everything changed. If Thornhill was indeed a spy, he would have had no reason to return to the estate after his escape the previous night, nor would the criminals be expecting him to return as they obviously did.

This was the only Hitchcock film released by [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"). Since 1986, it has been owned by [Turner Entertainment](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment"), as part of the pre-May 1986 MGM film library that it acquired through temporary ownership of MGM.

Production costs on *North by Northwest* rose when a delay in filming put Cary Grant into the penalty phase of his contract, resulting in his being paid an additional $5,000 per day before shooting even began.[[17]](#cite_note-spoto405-17)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Casting")]

[Eva Marie Saint's](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint") agent had told her that she had received an invitation to a dinner with Alfred Hitchcock and his family, the first time she and Hitchcock met. Days after the dinner, Saint's mother called her and reminded her that Hitchcock loved casting women wearing beige clothing and white gloves. Following her mother's advice, she met with Hitchcock again, wearing that combination. She credited this for helping her win the role.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18) MGM wanted [Cyd Charisse](/wiki/Cyd_Charisse "Cyd Charisse") for the role of Eve Kendall. Hitchcock stood by his choice of Saint.[[17]](#cite_note-spoto405-17)

Hitchcock attended the play *Middle of the Night* in order to watch a performance by [Edward G. Robinson](/wiki/Edward_G._Robinson "Edward G. Robinson"). After being impressed by the performance of [Martin Landau](/wiki/Martin_Landau "Martin Landau"), Hitchcock asked to meet him at [MGM](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"). Landau arrived and Hitchcock showed him the entirety of the project, including the storyboards. While they were looking at the project, Hitchcock turned and told Landau, "You're now Leonard."[[18]](#cite_note-:0-18)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_%2813%29.jpg/250px-North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_%2813%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_by_Northwest_movie_trailer_screenshot_(13).jpg)

The [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_headquarters "United Nations headquarters") is the location of a scene in the film

At Hitchcock's insistence, the film was made in Paramount's [VistaVision](/wiki/VistaVision "VistaVision") widescreen process. The only other VistaVision film made at MGM was *[High Society](/wiki/High_Society_(1956_film) "High Society (1956 film)")*.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The opening title sequence was created by graphic designer [Saul Bass](/wiki/Saul_Bass "Saul Bass"). *North by Northwest* was the first film to feature extended use of [kinetic typography](/wiki/Kinetic_typography "Kinetic typography") in its opening credits.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The aircraft flying in the aerial chase scene is a [Naval Aircraft Factory N3N](/wiki/Naval_Aircraft_Factory_N3N "Naval Aircraft Factory N3N") Canary, better known as the "Yellow Peril", a World War II Navy primary trainer sometimes converted for crop-dusting.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The aircraft that hits the truck and explodes is a wartime [Boeing-Stearman Model 75](/wiki/Boeing-Stearman_Model_75 "Boeing-Stearman Model 75") trainer, and many of these were used for agricultural purposes until the 1970s. The plane was piloted by Bob Coe, a crop-duster from [Wasco, California](/wiki/Wasco,_California "Wasco, California").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Hitchcock placed replicas of square Indiana highway signs in the scene. In 2000, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* ranked the crop-duster scene at No. 29 on their list of "The top 100 film moments".[[23]](#cite_note-23) The British film magazine *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* ranked it as the "greatest movie moment" of all time in its August 2009 issue.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Among the locations used in the film are:

* 430 Park Avenue [40°45′38″N 73°58′18″W﻿ / ﻿40.760694°N 73.971680°W﻿ / 40.760694; -73.971680](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.760694_N_73.97168_W_)
  + This is the building used by Bass during the opening credits. The building was constructed in 1916 as a luxury apartment tower called the Avenue Apartments and was designed by the firm [Warren and Wetmore](/wiki/Warren_and_Wetmore "Warren and Wetmore"). In 1953, the building was stripped of its façade, given a new curtain wall designed by [Emery Roth and Sons](/wiki/Emery_Roth "Emery Roth") in the style of [Lever House](/wiki/Lever_House "Lever House"), and converted to offices. Bass's title sequence is based on the geometric structure of the [international style](/wiki/International_Style_(architecture) "International Style (architecture)").[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* Commercial Investment Trust Building (650 Madison Avenue, New York) [40°45′50″N 73°58′17″W﻿ / ﻿40.763965°N 73.971302°W﻿ / 40.763965; -73.971302](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.763965_N_73.971302_W_)
  + This is the location of Roger Thornhill's office, and the building he walks out of in his first appearance in the film. The CIT Building was designed by the firm [Harrison and Abramovitz](/wiki/Harrison_%26_Abramovitz "Harrison & Abramovitz") and constructed in 1957.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* [Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Plaza_Hotel "Plaza Hotel") (768 Fifth Avenue, New York) [40°45′53″N 73°58′27″W﻿ / ﻿40.764733°N 73.974241°W﻿ / 40.764733; -73.974241](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.764733_N_73.974241_W_)
  + After taking a cab with his secretary, Thornhill has a drink in the [Oak Room](/wiki/Oak_Room_(Plaza_Hotel) "Oak Room (Plaza Hotel)") of the Plaza Hotel. It is here that he is kidnapped by Vandamm's henchmen. Thornhill later returns to the Plaza, where he breaks into George Kaplan's room.[[27]](#cite_note-27) For space reasons, most of the shots in and around the Oak Room were actually done on a set.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [Old Westbury Gardens](/wiki/Old_Westbury_Gardens "Old Westbury Gardens") (71 Old Westbury Road, Old Westbury) [40°46′26″N 73°35′42″W﻿ / ﻿40.773822°N 73.594921°W﻿ / 40.773822; -73.594921](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.773822_N_73.594921_W_)
  + Thornhill's kidnappers drive him to Townsend's estate on Long Island. After questioning Thornhill, Vandamm instructs Leonard and his other henchmen to intoxicate Thornhill by force.
* [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations") [40°44′57″N 73°58′06″W﻿ / ﻿40.749127°N 73.968327°W﻿ / 40.749127; -73.968327](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.749127_N_73.968327_W_)
  + Following Thornhill's escape from Vandamm's henchmen at the Plaza, he takes a taxi to the United Nations headquarters to meet Lester Townsend. The U.N. headquarters buildings were also designed by Harrison and Abramovitz, the architects of Thornhill's office.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The scene of Cary Grant going to the United Nations in New York was filmed illicitly because, after reviewing the script, U.N. authorities denied permission to film on or near its property. After two failed attempts to get the required shots, Hitchcock had Grant pull up in a taxicab right outside the General Assembly Building while a hidden camera crew filmed him exiting the vehicle and walking across the plaza.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") (89 East 42nd Street, New York) [40°45′10″N 73°58′38″W﻿ / ﻿40.752724°N 73.977221°W﻿ / 40.752724; -73.977221](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=40.752724_N_73.977221_W_)
  + Following the murder of Townsend at the United Nations, Thornhill rushes to Grand Central Terminal, where he sneaks onto the *[20th Century Limited](/wiki/20th_Century_Limited "20th Century Limited")* en route to Chicago.

  + Thornhill and Eve Kendall arrive in Chicago at the LaSalle Street Station. At the station, Kendall gives Thornhill the instructions for his meeting with Kaplan.
* Prairie Stop [35°45′39″N 119°33′42″W﻿ / ﻿35.760814°N 119.561721°W﻿ / 35.760814; -119.561721](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=35.760814_N_119.561721_W_)
  + The famous "crop duster scene", which in the film takes place in rural Indiana, was actually filmed on a highway in central California near the town of [Wasco](/wiki/Wasco,_California "Wasco, California"). Hitchcock added square signs to the location to replicate those found in Indiana.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* [Ambassador East Hotel](/wiki/Ambassador_East "Ambassador East") [41°54′21″N 87°37′42″W﻿ / ﻿41.905856°N 87.628303°W﻿ / 41.905856; -87.628303](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=41.905856_N_87.628303_W_)
  + Thornhill returns to Chicago in a stolen truck that he parks outside the Ambassador East Hotel. The hotel, designed by Robert S. DeGolyer and Co., opened in 1926. Today, it continues to be operated as a hotel, under the name The Ambassador.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* [Chicago Midway Airport](/wiki/Midway_International_Airport "Midway International Airport") [41°47′18″N 87°44′39″W﻿ / ﻿41.788372°N 87.744211°W﻿ / 41.788372; -87.744211](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=41.788372_N_87.744211_W_)
  + Following Thornhill's arrest at the auction, he and the Professor travel to Midway Airport, where they board a flight for Rapid City, South Dakota. The terminal seen in the film was built in 1945–46 and was designed by architect Paul Gerhardt Sr. This terminal building was demolished in 2002.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* Memorial View Building, [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") [43°52′37″N 103°27′22″W﻿ / ﻿43.877014°N 103.456213°W﻿ / 43.877014; -103.456213](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=North_by_Northwest&params=43.877014_N_103.456213_W_)
  + The spurious murder of Roger Thornhill takes place in the Buffalo Room of the Memorial View Building at Mount Rushmore—the one location in the park where Hitchcock was permitted to film.[[34]](#cite_note-Chapman-34) This building was constructed in 1957 as part of the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service")'s [Mission 66](/wiki/Mission_66 "Mission 66") program, and was designed jointly by NPS architect [Cecil J. Doty](/wiki/Cecil_J._Doty "Cecil J. Doty") and local architect [Harold Spitznagel](/wiki/Harold_Spitznagel "Harold Spitznagel"). The building was demolished in 1994.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Vandamm House](/wiki/Vandamm_House "Vandamm House")
  + Vandamm's house, set on a cliff atop Mount Rushmore, was not a real structure. Hitchcock asked the set designers to make the house in the style of [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright")—at the time the most popular architect in the United States—using the materials, form, and interiors associated with him.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Set designer [Robert F. Boyle](/wiki/Robert_F._Boyle "Robert F. Boyle") planned the house, which featured a cantilevered living room and made extensive use of limestone. Exterior shots were done using [matte paintings](/wiki/Matte_painting "Matte painting"),[[37]](#cite_note-Looking_at_Movies:_An_Introduction_to_Film2-37) while interior shots were filmed using a set built in [Culver City, California](/wiki/Culver_City,_California "Culver City, California"), where MGM's studios were located.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Costuming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Costuming")]

A panel of fashion experts convened by *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* in 2006 said the gray suit worn by Cary Grant throughout almost the entire film was the best suit in film history, and the most influential on men's style, stating that it has since been copied for [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise")'s character in *[Collateral](/wiki/Collateral_(film) "Collateral (film)")* and [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck")'s character in *[Paycheck](/wiki/Paycheck_(film) "Paycheck (film)")*.[[39]](#cite_note-39) This sentiment has been echoed by writer [Todd McEwen](/wiki/Todd_McEwen "Todd McEwen"), who called it "gorgeous" and wrote a short story, "Cary Grant's Suit", that recounts the film's plot, featuring the suit.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-LH-41)

There is some disagreement as to who tailored the suit; *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* magazine claimed it was [Norton & Sons](/wiki/Norton_%26_Sons "Norton & Sons") of London,[[42]](#cite_note-42) although according to *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*, it was Quintino of [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills "Beverly Hills").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Another article states that Grant used his [Savile Row tailor](/wiki/Savile_Row_tailoring "Savile Row tailoring"), [Kilgour French and Stanbury](/wiki/Savile_Row_tailoring#Kilgour,_French_&_Stanbury "Savile Row tailoring") for the suit. A label reading "Quintino" is visible on one of the suits in the film, but this is because Quintino made duplicate suits for scenes involving more activity or stunts.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Eva Marie Saint's wardrobe for the film was originally entirely chosen by MGM. Hitchcock disliked MGM's selections, and the actress and director went to [Bergdorf Goodman](/wiki/Bergdorf_Goodman "Bergdorf Goodman") in New York to select what she would wear.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Editing and post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Editing and post-production")]

In [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut")'s book-length interview, *Hitchcock/Truffaut* (1967), Hitchcock said that MGM wanted *North by Northwest* cut by 15 minutes so the film's length would run under two hours. Hitchcock had his agent check his contract, learned that he had absolute control over the final cut, and refused.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

One of Eva Marie Saint's lines in the dining-car seduction scene was redubbed. She originally said, "I never make love on an empty stomach", but it was changed in post-production to "I never discuss love on an empty stomach", as the censors considered the original version too risqué.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Release")]

Trailer for *North by Northwest*

The film opened on July 1, 1959, at the [United Artists Theatre](/wiki/United_Artists_Theatre_(Chicago) "United Artists Theatre (Chicago)") in Chicago.[[1]](#cite_note-Chicago-1) It had a seven-week run at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") in August and September 1959. One trailer for *North by Northwest* features Hitchcock presenting himself as the owner of Alfred Hitchcock Travel Agency and telling the viewer he has made a motion picture to advertise these wonderful vacation stops.[[49]](#cite_note-49) A new [70mm](/wiki/70_mm_film "70 mm film") restoration of the film, remastered in 13K resolution from the original VistaVision elements, premiered at the 2024 [Tribeca Festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Festival "Tribeca Festival") on June 12, 2024, to celebrate the film's 65th anniversary.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Home media")]

*North by Northwest* was released on the [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") Disc format in the United States in November 2009, by [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") with a [1080p](/wiki/1080p "1080p") [VC-1](/wiki/VC-1 "VC-1") encoding.[[52]](#cite_note-IGN-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Wired.com-53) This release is a special 50th-anniversary edition, restored and remastered from original VistaVision elements. A DVD edition was also released. A 65th anniversary [4K Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") was released in 2024 by [Warner Bros. Home Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery_Home_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Discovery Home Entertainment").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Box office")]

In its opening in Chicago, it grossed $46,000 in its first week and $35,000 the second week.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The film grossed $209,000 in its opening week at Radio City Music Hall, setting a record opening week at the theater, as well as its record non-holiday week gross, and went on to gross a record $404,056 in two weeks.[[56]](#cite_note-auto-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Its opening at the Music Hall saw it become the [number one film at the US box office](/wiki/List_of_1959_box_office_number-one_films_in_the_United_States "List of 1959 box office number-one films in the United States"), where it remained for its seven weeks at the Music Hall.[[58]](#cite_note-58) By the end of August, it had grossed $2,568,000 from 139 engagements ($28.4 million in 2025 dollars[[59]](#cite_note-inflation-US-59)).[[60]](#cite_note-60)

According to MGM records, the film earned [theatrical rentals](/wiki/Theatrical_rental "Theatrical rental") of $5,740,000 in the United States and Canada and $4.1 million elsewhere, resulting in a profit of $837,000.[[2]](#cite_note-Mannix-2)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

A contemporary in *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* called the film "smoothly troweled and thoroughly entertaining".[[61]](#cite_note-61) A. H. Weiler of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* made it a "Critic's Pick" and said it was the "year's most scenic, intriguing and merriest chase"; he also complimented the two leads:

> Cary Grant, a veteran member of the Hitchcock acting varsity, was never more at home than in this role of the advertising-man-on-the-lam. He handles the grimaces, the surprised look, the quick smile, ... and all the derring-do with professional aplomb and grace, In casting Eva Marie Saint as his romantic vis-à-vis, Mr. Hitchcock has plumbed some talents not shown by the actress heretofore. Although she is seemingly a hard, designing type, she also emerges both the sweet heroine and a glamorous charmer.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Period film critic [Charles Champlin](/wiki/Charles_Champlin "Charles Champlin") saw the film as an "anthology of typical Hitchcockian situations" and was particularly taken by the scene and suspense in which Grant's character avoids death when attacked by a crop-dusting plane in the cornfields, which he believed was representative of Hitchcock's finest work.[[63]](#cite_note-63) *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* critic [Penelope Houston](/wiki/Penelope_Houston_(film_critic) "Penelope Houston (film critic)") called it "the purest piece of entertainment filmmaking".[[64]](#cite_note-64) In 2002 author and journalist [Nick Clooney](/wiki/Nick_Clooney "Nick Clooney") praised Lehman's original story and sophisticated dialogue, calling the film "certainly Alfred Hitchcock's most stylish thriller, if not his best".[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The London edition of *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")*, reviewing the film in 2008, commented:

> Fifty years on, you could say that Hitchcock's sleek, wry, paranoid thriller caught the zeitgeist perfectly: Cold War shadiness, secret agents of power, urbane modernism, the ant-like bustle of city life, and a hint of dread behind the sharp suits of affluence. Cary Grant's Roger Thornhill, the film's sharply dressed ad exec who is sucked into a vortex of mistaken identity, certainly wouldn't be out of place in *[Mad Men](/wiki/Mad_Men "Mad Men")*. But there's nothing dated about this perfect storm of talent, from Hitchcock and Grant to writer Ernest Lehman (*Sweet Smell of Success*), co-stars James Mason and Eva Marie Saint, composer Bernard Herrmann and even designer Saul Bass, whose opening-credits sequence still manages to send a shiver down the spine.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

*[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* ranked *North by Northwest* at No. 49 in its "Top 250 Best Films of the Century" list in 1999, based on a poll of critics.[[67]](#cite_note-67) *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* voted it the 44th-greatest film of all time in 1999.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The film was voted at No. 28 on the list of "100 Greatest Films" by the prominent French magazine *[Cahiers du cinéma](/wiki/Cahiers_du_cin%C3%A9ma "Cahiers du cinéma")* in 2008.[[69]](#cite_note-:2-69) In 2010, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* ranked it as the second-best action and war film of all time.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The film ranks at No. 98 in *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")'*s 2011 list of the 500 Greatest Films of All Time.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s 2012 *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* polls of [the greatest films ever made](/wiki/The_Sight_%26_Sound_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time_2012 "The Sight & Sound Greatest Films of All Time 2012"), *North by Northwest* was ranked 53rd among critics;[[72]](#cite_note-72)

As of 2025 *North by Northwest* holds a 97% rating on the review aggregator [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), based on 119 reviews, with an average rating of 9.3/10. The site states the critical consensus as: "Gripping, suspenseful and visually iconic, this late-period Hitchcock classic laid the groundwork for countless action thrillers to follow."[[73]](#cite_note-73) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), it has a score of 98 out of 100, based on reviews from 16 critics.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 1998, *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* conducted a poll, and *North by Northwest* was voted the twelfth greatest film of all time.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

*North by Northwest* was ranked 13th in [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s 2015 list of the 100 greatest American films.[[76]](#cite_note-76) In 2022, *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* named *North by Northwest* the greatest thriller film ever made.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In 2006, the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") ranked the screenplay No. 21 on its 2020 list of 101 Greatest Screenplays ever written.[[78]](#cite_note-78) It is ranked the 40th-greatest American film by the American Film Institute.[[79]](#cite_note-79) [The Sight and Sound Greatest Films of All Time 2022 poll](/wiki/The_Sight_and_Sound_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time_2022 "The Sight and Sound Greatest Films of All Time 2022") ranked the film 45th among critics.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Awards")]

*North by Northwest* was nominated for three [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")—[Best Film Editing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing") ([George Tomasini](/wiki/George_Tomasini "George Tomasini")), [Best Art Direction – Set Decoration, Color](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Production_Design "Academy Award for Best Production Design") ([William A. Horning](/wiki/William_A._Horning "William A. Horning"), [Robert F. Boyle](/wiki/Robert_F._Boyle "Robert F. Boyle"), [Merrill Pye](/wiki/Merrill_Pye "Merrill Pye"), [Henry Grace](/wiki/Henry_Grace "Henry Grace"), and [Frank R. McKelvy](/wiki/Frank_R._McKelvy "Frank R. McKelvy")), and [Best Original Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay") ([Ernest Lehman](/wiki/Ernest_Lehman "Ernest Lehman"))—at the [32nd Academy Awards](/wiki/32nd_Academy_Awards "32nd Academy Awards") ceremony.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Two of the three awards went instead to *[Ben-Hur](/wiki/Ben-Hur_(1959_film) "Ben-Hur (1959 film)")*, and the other went to *[Pillow Talk](/wiki/Pillow_Talk_(film) "Pillow Talk (film)")*. The film—and Lehman specifically—also won a 1960 [Edgar Award](/wiki/Edgar_Award "Edgar Award") for Best Motion Picture Screenplay. Hitchcock received his second [Silver Shell for Best Director](/wiki/Silver_Shell_for_Best_Director "Silver Shell for Best Director") award at the [San Sebastián International Film Festival](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_International_Film_Festival "San Sebastián International Film Festival"). (He was also awarded the Silver Shell the year before for *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")*.)[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In 1995, *North by Northwest* was selected for preservation in the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the United States [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") as being "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant." In June 2008, the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") revealed its "[10 Top 10](/wiki/AFI%27s_10_Top_10 "AFI's 10 Top 10")"—the best 10 films in 10 "classic" American film genres—after polling over 1,500 people from the creative community. *North by Northwest* was acknowledged as the seventh-best film in the mystery genre.[[84]](#cite_note-afi1010-84) It was also listed as No. 40 in [AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Movies "AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies"),[[85]](#cite_note-85) No. 4 in [AFI's 100 Years...100 Thrills](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Thrills "AFI's 100 Years...100 Thrills"),[[86]](#cite_note-86) and No. 55 in [AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies (10th Anniversary Edition)](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Movies_(10th_Anniversary_Edition) "AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies (10th Anniversary Edition)").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

## Themes and motifs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Themes and motifs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg/250px-North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_by_Northwest_stars_pose_at_Rushmore.jpg)

[James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason"), [Eva Marie Saint](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint") and [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant") at [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") during filming. Studio [mockups](/wiki/Mockup "Mockup") were intercut with actual monument footage for the climactic scene.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Nxnwsign.jpg/250px-Nxnwsign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nxnwsign.jpg)

Sign near Mount Rushmore

Hitchcock planned the film as a change of pace after his dark romantic thriller *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* a year earlier. In his book-length interview *Hitchcock/Truffaut* (1967) with [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut"), Hitchcock said that he wanted to do "something fun, light-hearted, and generally free of the symbolism permeating his other movies."[[88]](#cite_note-88) Writer Ernest Lehman has mocked those who look for symbolism in the film.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In addition to its popular appeal, the film is considered to be a masterpiece for its themes of [deception](/wiki/Deception "Deception"), [mistaken identity](/wiki/Mistaken_identity_(plot_device) "Mistaken identity (plot device)"), and [moral relativism](/wiki/Moral_relativism "Moral relativism") in the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") era.

Some[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] have mistaken the title *North by Northwest* as having come from a line in *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] ("I am but mad north-north-west: when the wind is southerly I know a hawk from a handsaw"), a work also concerned with the shifty nature of reality.[[90]](#cite_note-90) However, Hitchcock explained in an interview with [Peter Bogdanovich](/wiki/Peter_Bogdanovich "Peter Bogdanovich") in 1963: "It's a fantasy. The whole film is epitomized in the title—there is no such thing as north-by-northwest on the compass."[[91]](#cite_note-91) (The similar "northwest by north" is indeed one of 32 [points of the compass](/wiki/Points_of_the_compass "Points of the compass").)

Lehman states that he used a working title for the film of *In a Northwesterly Direction* because the film's action was to begin in New York and climax in Alaska. Then the head of the story department at MGM suggested *North by Northwest*, but this was still to be a working title—shared with the public as early as June 1958.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Other titles were considered, including *The Man on Lincoln's Nose*, but *North by Northwest* was kept because, according to Lehman, "We never did find a [better] title."[[14]](#cite_note-brady201-14) The [Northwest Airlines](/wiki/Northwest_Airlines "Northwest Airlines") was worked into the film, reinforcing the title.

The film's plot involves a "[MacGuffin](/wiki/MacGuffin "MacGuffin")"—a term popularized by Hitchcock—which is a physical object that everyone in the film is chasing, but which has no deep relationship to the plot. Late in *North by Northwest*, it emerges that the spies are attempting to smuggle [microfilm](/wiki/Microform "Microform") containing government secrets out of the country. The film's protagonist, Roger Thornhill, only becomes involved through a case of mistaken identity (with a spy that does not even exist, "George Kaplan"), and only becomes a target for elimination when he becomes a persistent nuisance as a result.

*North by Northwest* has been referred to as "the first [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") film"[[93]](#cite_note-93) because of its splashily colorful settings, secret agents, and an elegant, daring, wisecracking leading man opposite a sinister yet strangely charming villain. The crop-duster scene inspired the helicopter chase in *[From Russia with Love](/wiki/From_Russia_with_Love_(film) "From Russia with Love (film)")*.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

The film's final shot—that of the train speeding into a tunnel during a romantic embrace onboard—is a famous bit of self-conscious [Freudian symbolism](/wiki/Free_association_(psychology) "Free association (psychology)") reflecting Hitchcock's mischievous sense of humor. In the book *Hitchcock/Truffaut* (pp. 107–108), Hitchcock called it a "phallic symbol ... probably one of the most impudent shots I ever made".

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Influences")]

The film was very influential on the [James Bond films](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") and subsequent action-thriller films, as well as the TV series *[The Man from U.N.C.L.E.](/wiki/The_Man_from_U.N.C.L.E. "The Man from U.N.C.L.E.")*, where [Leo G. Carroll](/wiki/Leo_G._Carroll "Leo G. Carroll") played the same sort of role as the head of a top secret intelligence agency.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

The film's title is reported to have been the influence for the name of the popular annual live-music festival [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest") in Austin, Texas, started in 1987, with the name idea coming from Louis Black, editor and co-founder of the local alternative weekly *[The Austin Chronicle](/wiki/The_Austin_Chronicle "The Austin Chronicle")*, as a play on the Hitchcock film title.[[98]](#cite_note-sxsw_stays_course-98)

The third episode of the [14th season](/wiki/Doctor_Who_(season_14) "Doctor Who (season 14)") of *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* serial "[The Deadly Assassin](/wiki/The_Deadly_Assassin "The Deadly Assassin")" (1976) includes an homage to *North by Northwest*, when the Doctor, who like Hitchcock's hero is falsely accused of a politically motivated murder, is attacked by gunfire from a biplane piloted by one of his enemy's henchmen.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

The 2010 Spanish dark comedy film *[The Last Circus](/wiki/The_Last_Circus "The Last Circus")* pays visual homage to the [Mount Rushmore scene](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore_in_popular_culture#In_North_by_Northwest "Mount Rushmore in popular culture") in its climactic [scene atop a controversial Francoist monument](/wiki/Valley_of_the_Fallen#In_popular_culture "Valley of the Fallen").[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)

## Adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_by_Northwest&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Adaptations")]

*North by Northwest* was adapted as a stage play by Carolyn Burns. The adaptation premiered at the [Melbourne Theatre Company](/wiki/Melbourne_Theatre_Company "Melbourne Theatre Company") in 2015.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Another stage adaptation, adapted and directed by [Emma Rice](/wiki/Emma_Rice "Emma Rice"), opened at the [York Theatre Royal](/wiki/York_Theatre_Royal "York Theatre Royal") in March 2025 before touring the UK, ending with a run at the [Alexandra Palace Theatre](/wiki/Alexandra_Palace_Theatre "Alexandra Palace Theatre"), [London](/wiki/London "London").[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)
